dogma_adjustments: Rule 11: Industry structure fit - NRSRO designation is a legally mandated government designation with only 3 holders (S&P, Moody's, Fitch). This is the strongest form of Cornered Resource: regulatory enforcement of oligopoly.
Rule 4: Highly quantified - 43.8% adjusted operating margin (2014), 46.3% Q2 2014, 8% revenue growth, 13% profit growth, 190bps margin expansion.
Rule 3: There are exactly 2 comparable competitors (Moody's and Fitch). The oligopolistic structure with regulatory entry barriers eliminates competition.
scored_claims.scoring_rationale: MHFI-001 is the clearest regulatory Cornered Resource in the chunk_11 universe, second only to LMT-001 (F-35 prime contractor). NRSRO designation creates legally mandated demand from regulated financial institutions. Pattern IV and Pattern I both apply at maximum strength. The 43-46% operating margins confirm the pricing power that only a regulated oligopoly can sustain. Score confirmed at 0.9.
scored_claims.cagr_connection: Very strong: corporate debt issuance (transaction revenue) + recurring subscription revenue from investors and institutions creates dual CAGR engines. Growing European corporate bond market (companies shifting from bank financing to capital markets) adds an incremental growth driver.

dogma_adjustments: Rule 11: Industry structure fit - index licensing is a textbook network economy: more AUM tracking an index → more investor familiarity → more AUM adoption → more licensing fees. S&P 500 dominance is self-reinforcing.
Rule 4: Highly quantified - ETF AUM $832B (+25% YoY), mutual fund AUM >$1T, 63.6% operating margin, every incremental dollar of revenue = $1 operating profit.
Rule 3: MSCI and FTSE are pure competitors. S&P Dow Jones Indices' position in US equity (S&P 500, S&P 400, S&P 600) is dominant vs MSCI's relative strength in international/EM.
scored_claims.scoring_rationale: Network Economies in index licensing are real and powerful: once S&P 500 became the benchmark, all US equity products are measured against it, all derivatives require it, all passive funds track it. This creates an entrenched network where switching the benchmark would require coordinating across thousands of fund managers and institutional investors simultaneously. 63.6% operating margin with 100% revenue-to-profit flow demonstrates the network economy power. Score confirmed at 0.9.
scored_claims.cagr_connection: Very strong: passive investing secular trend directly compounds AUM-based licensing fees. Record $331B ETF inflows in 2014 is the most direct CAGR driver. Network effect means each new ETF launch tracking S&P indices compounds the AUM base further.

dogma_adjustments: Rule 4: 8% desktop revenue growth and 11% increase in Desktop revenue are quantified. 4 consecutive quarters of adjusted profit growth after 2-year investment confirms trajectory.

Rule 3: Bloomberg Terminal is the dominant pure competitor. PresCenter (Office integration) is a differentiation attempt, but Bloomberg has stronger market position in financial professional workflows.
Rule 7: Against Bloomberg, the switching costs in Capital IQ are weaker than Bloomberg's own switching costs. This is a #2 position in enterprise financial data.
scored_claims.scoring_rationale: Pattern III (capability over result) applies - PresCenter workflow integration is a capability description. However, Rule 7 (pure competitor differentiation) identifies that Bloomberg is the dominant market leader and Capital IQ is the #2 platform. The switching costs are real but less strong than Bloomberg's embedded terminal culture. Score maintained at 0.5.
scored_claims.cagr_connection: Moderate: growing enterprise customer base + increasing revenue per user through expanded data services provides steady CAGR. 8% desktop revenue growth supports this.

dogma_adjustments: Rule 4: High single-digit organic revenue growth in 2014 despite dramatically falling oil prices demonstrates price inelasticity. Petroleum segment strong despite commodity price collapse.
Rule 11: Industry structure fit - commodity price benchmarks are natural monopolies within their commodity category. Physical commodity contracts embed Platts prices as legal reference benchmarks.
Rule 3: Platts, Argus, and ICIS are the three dominant commodity pricing services. Within petroleum, Platts has the strongest market position.
scored_claims.scoring_rationale: Platts commodity price assessment is a Cornered Resource: commodity markets need a single reference price for contract settlement, and Platts provides this for petroleum, metals, and agriculture. Growing despite collapsing oil prices confirms the inelastic demand - physical traders still need the reference price even when prices fall dramatically. Pattern IV (structural market position) and Pattern I (high single-digit growth despite commodity price collapse) both apply strongly. Score maintained at 0.7.
scored_claims.cagr_connection: Strong: commodity market complexity (derivatives growth, physical trade expansion) drives demand for price transparency. Metals & Agriculture at 34% growth in 2014 shows category diversification.

dogma_adjustments: Rule 4: Record $331B ETF inflows in 2014 is third-party quantified data (ETF industry statistics). 10% CAGR in revenue, 24% EPS CAGR over 3 years.
Rule 5: CAGR mechanism is direct and simple: passive investing → more AUM → higher AUM-based fees → revenue growth. Pattern II (direct CAGR mechanism) applies.
Rule 11: Passive investing is not just an industry common trend - S&P Dow Jones Indices specifically benefits above all peers given the S&P 500's dominance as the benchmark.
scored_claims.scoring_rationale: This is a CAGR connection claim with the strongest evidence in chunk_11. Pattern II (direct CAGR mechanism) applies: passive investing secular trend → ETF AUM growth → AUM-based licensing fee revenue. Third-party data ($331B ETF inflows) confirms the trend. S&P specifically benefits above-proportionally given the S&P 500's market dominance. Score maintained at 0.7.
scored_claims.cagr_connection: Very strong: passive investing is a multi-decade secular trend that directly compounds AUM-based licensing revenue. European corporate bond market expansion adds a second CAGR engine through Ratings growth.
